claim_status: No-claim engine-landscape source observations only; these sources do not select an engine architecture, approve a dependency or source relationship, establish comparative performance, compatibility, security, accessibility, or Chrome-class status, or authorize implementation.

sources.title: RenderingNG architecture
sources.observation: Chromium documents a staged rendering pipeline from animation and style through layout, paint, raster, activation, aggregation, and drawing.
sources.decision_consequence: Use explicit pipeline stages, invalidation, ownership, and trace boundaries as hypotheses; do not treat the architecture as proof of Turing performance.

sources.title: Process model and site isolation
sources.observation: Chromium describes browser, renderer, utility, GPU, and site-isolation process roles and their security implications.
sources.decision_consequence: Retain process authority and site identity as measured design inputs; require equivalent security and failure evidence before comparisons.

sources.title: Chrome DevTools Protocol
sources.observation: CDP exposes a broad domain and command/event model for browser inspection and automation.
sources.decision_consequence: Study protocol breadth and generation while keeping Turing's internal protocol independently versioned and security-bounded.

sources.title: Sparkplug baseline compiler
sources.observation: V8 documents a fast baseline compilation tier between interpretation and higher optimization.
sources.decision_consequence: Treat tiering as an experiment requiring warmup, code-size, equivalence, and deoptimization measurements.

sources.title: Maglev mid-tier compiler
sources.observation: V8 documents a mid-tier compiler aimed at useful optimization with lower compilation cost than peak optimization.
sources.decision_consequence: Compare tier cost and interaction benefit only through controlled runtime and end-to-end workloads.

sources.title: WebKit documentation
sources.observation: WebKit's documentation connects architecture, testing, ports, and platform integration.
sources.decision_consequence: Keep common engine contracts separate from replaceable platform adapters and test infrastructure.

sources.title: WebKit2 architecture
sources.observation: WebKit2 separates UI, web-content, and shared privileged services.
sources.decision_consequence: Use brokered privileged operations as an architectural hypothesis, not as evidence of a safe Turing boundary.

sources.title: WebKit site isolation
sources.observation: WebKit describes remote frames, browsing-context groups, provisional navigation, and cross-process coordination.
sources.decision_consequence: Preserve document, frame, site, and epoch identity across process boundaries.

sources.title: JavaScriptCore overview
sources.observation: JavaScriptCore uses interpreter and tiered execution stages.
sources.decision_consequence: Use tier boundaries and semantic-oracle requirements in the JavaScript research plan.

sources.title: Firefox process model
sources.observation: Firefox documents specialized parent, content, GPU, socket, media, service-worker, and other process roles.
sources.decision_consequence: Evaluate specialized roles against launch, memory, scheduling, and blast-radius costs rather than assuming more processes are better.

sources.title: SpiderMonkey overview
sources.observation: SpiderMonkey documents interpretation, baseline and optimizing compilation, garbage collection, and WebAssembly as distinct systems.
sources.decision_consequence: Measure runtime tier state, GC, code retention, and WebAssembly behavior as separate evidence dimensions.

sources.title: Firefox performance documentation
sources.observation: Firefox provides documentation for profiling, memory, power, heap, allocation, and process analysis.
sources.decision_consequence: Treat resource observability as a product and diagnostic requirement, not a one-off benchmark aid.

sources.title: Servo goals and governance
sources.observation: Servo presents a Rust-based, modular, embeddable engine project with independent governance and standards goals.
sources.decision_consequence: Use Servo for architecture and governance comparison only; no source, component, or upstream relationship is approved.

sources.title: Ladybird project
sources.observation: Ladybird presents an independent, greenfield browser and publicly reports ongoing engine, sandbox, GPU, and compatibility work.
sources.decision_consequence: Use its visible sequencing as a comparison input while retaining Turing's stricter evidence and security gates.

sources.title: Web Platform Tests
sources.observation: WPT provides a shared cross-browser conformance and interoperability test corpus.
sources.decision_consequence: Require standards tests and full failure denominators before compatibility conclusions; WPT presence is not a result.

sources.title: WebDriver BiDi
sources.observation: WebDriver BiDi defines a standards-facing bidirectional automation protocol.
sources.decision_consequence: Use standards-facing automation as a separate compatibility layer from deep engine diagnostics.

sources.title: Interop 2026
sources.observation: Interop prioritizes shared web-platform focus areas across browser engines.
sources.decision_consequence: Use focus-area selection to shape compatibility research, not to infer product parity.

sources.title: BrowserBench
sources.observation: BrowserBench provides synthetic suites that can diagnose selected web workloads under declared conditions.
sources.decision_consequence: Pin suite versions and retain raw per-workload results; synthetic scores cannot establish a number-one claim.

unsupported: No engine, source relationship, dependency, toolkit, or architecture is selected by this manifest.
No Turing or competitor browser run, benchmark result, compatibility result, security result, or accessibility result is recorded.
No fastest, lower-memory, lower-energy, Chrome-class, production, or number-one claim is supported.
No source code is approved for copying or release use by these observations.
